title: Welcome to Engineering (AI) Agents
author: Russ Miles
text:
Welcome to Engineering (AI) Agents
Our goals and aims for this new blog
TL; DR
There is a paucity of developer-focussed guidance, samples and examples of how to engineer great AI agents.
The job of this blog: To explore, explain and develop as many free and open source articles, samples and examples of real agent development as possible.
Code over promises is the goal.
If you’re interested in getting involved get in touch here on Substack, LinkedIn or even over on GitHub (the location of all the working code samples and examples).
Promises, promises everywhere, but very little code…
That may not be a complete statement of fact, but for me it was very close a few weeks ago. After being asked what workshop I’d suggest for a local Business+Tech conference (see: Eastbourne Digifest) I sat back and pondered what code I was dying to write. What was I missing? What did other people find missing too?
Then Embabel dropped and it woke me up with a jolt.
Developing AI agents was tricky enough a domain that Rod Johnson, someone I really admire, had seen fit to codify a new programming model into a new open source project. Very exciting times!
Of course there are a multitude of AI agent frameworks out there, but this was the first one I’d seen that just shouted “Regular developers are going to be building these things! And so we can make that easier.” Just as the Spring framework used to help me do the right things easily — and other things if I needed — so Embabel planted a flag that read that AI agent development was for all software developers, including me. It always had been I suppose, but I really appreciated the wake up call.
And then I hit a sticking point. There is a metric tonne of material out there on what agents might be able to do, and the challenges they practically face, but so, so little guidance, advice, examples or samples of how to develop agents. I really struggled to find much at all that spoke to my developer sensitivities. I found lots of snippets but very little source; lots of fluff but very little stuff.
Where was all the code examples to help me develop AI agents (with Embabel and beyond)? There are lots of small samples, but explanations of what they are and how they work can be fairly tricky to approach and feel confident with. And not always because these frameworks are as new as Embabel either! As a software developer working in an Enterprise I found very little help that spoke to how to professionally build agents, where every piece of code I write can potentially be an agent because it could benefit from integrating with multiple systems, sources and tools, with wildly varying veracity of responses.
Writing AI agents has become a daily exercise for me but the paucity of developer-focussed guidance and examples has been a constant complaint. Not any more.
That’s the job of this blog: To explore, explain and develop as many free and open source articles, samples and examples of real agent development as possible.
Code over promises is the goal!
If you’re interested in getting involved, either with the code or even writing for this blog, then please get in touch either by pinging me here on Substack, LinkedIn or even over on GitHub (the location of all the working code samples and examples).
Even if you just have requests for topics or samples we could cover, pop them as a GitHub ticket or fire me an email.
Let’s create a smorgasbord of great articles and code samples and examples for developers who are taking their first, second, and even hundredth steps into building their own AI agents.
And, at the same time, let’s have some fun!
Be kind,
Russ
